elements.sent-2411-001: Open-set Domain Adaptation (OSDA) aims to adapt a model from a labeled source domain to an unlabeled target domain, where novel classes — also referred to as target-private unknown classes — are present.
elements.sent-2411-002: Source-free Open-set Domain Adaptation (SF-OSDA) methods address OSDA without accessing labeled source data, making them particularly relevant under privacy constraints.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, SF-OSDA presents significant challenges due to distribution shifts and the introduction of novel classes.
elements.sent-2411-004: Existing SF-OSDA methods typically rely on thresholding the prediction entropy of a sample to identify it as either a known or unknown class but fail to explicitly learn discriminative features for the target-private unknown classes.
elements.sent-2411-005: We propose Recall and Refine (RRDA), a novel SF-OSDA framework designed to address these limitations by explicitly learning features for target-private unknown classes.
elements.sent-2411-007: First, we enhance the model’s capacity to recognize unknown classes by training a target classifier with an additional decision boundary, guided by synthetic samples generated from target domain features.
elements.sent-2411-008: This enables the classifier to effectively separate known and unknown classes.
elements.sent-2411-009: In the second step, we adapt the entire model to the target domain, addressing both domain shifts and improving generalization to unknown classes.
elements.sent-2411-010: Any off-the-shelf source-free domain adaptation method (e.g., SHOT, AaD) can be seamlessly integrated into our framework at this stage.
elements.sent-2411-011: Extensive experiments on three benchmark datasets demonstrate that RRDA significantly outperforms existing SF-OSDA and OSDA methods.
elements.sent-2411-013: Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (UDA)~\cite{ben2010theory,ganin2015unsupervised,long2015learning adapts a model from a labeled source domain to an unlabeled target domain~\cite{oza2023unsupervised , effectively addressing the issue of domain shift where the source and target distributions differ.}}
elements.sent-2411-014: UDA strategies typically align feature distributions between domains using metric learning techniques~\cite{long2015learning,kang2019contrastive or adversarial training~\cite{ganin2015unsupervised, tzeng2017adversarial,luo2019taking , and more recently, self-training approaches~\cite{sun2022safe, Hoyer_2023_CVPR, Zhu_2023_CVPR .}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Despite their success, most current domain adaptation approaches operate under the assumption of a shared label set between the source and target domains (i.e., $C_s = C_t$), referred to as Closed-set Domain Adaptation~\cite{saenko2010adapting .}
elements.sent-2411-016: However, this assumption is often impractical in real-world scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-017: In contrast, Open-set Domain Adaptation (OSDA) extends the target label space beyond that of the source domain (i.e., $C_s \subset C_t$)~\cite{saito2018open,liu2019separate , thereby adding complexity to the DA task.}
elements.sent-2411-018: OSDA aims to align target samples from known classes with those from the source domain while effectively identifying target samples belonging to categories not observed in the source domain, referred to as unknown classes~\cite{panareda2017open,bucci2020effectiveness,jang2022unknown .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Various criteria based on instance-level predictions have been proposed, including entropy-based~\cite{feng2021open,saito2020universal and confidence-based~\cite{saito2021ovanet,fu2020learning methods.}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Additionally, privacy and legal considerations increasingly limit access to labeled source data for adaptation purposes.
elements.sent-2411-021: To address this, source-free adaptation methods~\cite{fang2024source have emerged, enabling adaptation without reliance on labeled source data~\cite{kim2021domain,kundu2020universal,li2020model .}}
elements.sent-2411-022: In this paper, we focus on Source-free Open-set Domain Adaptation (SF-OSDA), where only a pre-trained source model is available for knowledge transfer, without access to labeled source data.
elements.sent-2411-023: While some Source-free Domain Adaptation (SF-DA) methods have demonstrated effectiveness in addressing SF-OSDA for classification tasks~\cite{liang2020we,yang2022attracting,wan2024unveiling , semantic segmentation~\cite{choe2024open , and graph applications~\cite{wang2024open , they primarily focus on the semantics of known classes in the source domain, often overlooking the crucial aspect of novel-class semantics.}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: These methods focus on segregating target samples with low entropy, categorizing them as known classes, and subsequently optimizing specific objectives such as entropy minimization or clustering.
elements.sent-2411-025: In this process, data points associated with known classes are prioritized, while those with high entropy are typically excluded from training, leading to a semantic disparity between the known and unknown classes.
elements.sent-2411-026: To effectively adapt a pre-trained source model to a target domain facing both category and distribution shifts, we propose Recall and Refine for Domain Adaptation (RRDA) for robust SF-OSDA.
elements.sent-2411-028: First, we propose to leverage the semantics of the unknown classes by introducing a novel target classifier with $K+K'$ decision boundaries.
elements.sent-2411-029: These boundaries extend the $K$ classes from the source domain with $K'$ additional classes for the unknown categories.
elements.sent-2411-030: To achieve this, synthetic samples are generated in the feature space from target domain features.
elements.sent-2411-031: These synthetic points are optimized to exhibit low entropy for known classes and high entropy for unknown classes, which are then clustered into $K'$ categories.
elements.sent-2411-032: The synthetic data are used to refine the decision boundaries of the source classifier, enabling the target classifier to accommodate the unknown classes.
elements.sent-2411-033: In the second step, any off-the-shelf source-free domain adaptation method (e.g., SHOT~\cite{liang2020we , AaD~\cite{yang2022attracting ) can be integrated into our framework to adapt the entire model to the target domain.}}
elements.sent-2411-034: RRDA directly learns to classify target unknown classes.
elements.sent-2411-035: The framework introduces $K'$ as a hyper-parameter, which we set to $K' = K$ for simplicity.
elements.sent-2411-036: Sensitivity analysis shows that performance improves with higher values of $K'$, though results remain robust across a range of settings.
elements.sent-2411-037: Extensive experiments on three SF-OSDA benchmark datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach, significantly outperforming existing methods.
elements.sent-2411-038: Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (UDA) aims to adapt a model originally trained on a labeled source domain to perform effectively in an unlabeled target domain.
elements.sent-2411-039: This adaptation process assumes access to data from both the source and target domains during training~\cite{oza2023unsupervised .}
elements.sent-2411-040: UDA strategies often align feature distributions between domains using metric learning techniques~\cite{long2015learning,kang2019contrastive,nejjar2023dare or adversarial training across various spaces, including image input space~\cite{murez2018image,pizzati2020domain , feature space~\cite{ganin2015unsupervised , and output space~\cite{luo2019taking,vu2019dada .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-041: Additionally, various techniques incorporate pseudo-labeling or self-training algorithms~\cite{sun2022safe,dong2023SimMMDG,yue2024make , which generate pseudo-labels for unlabeled samples in the target domain.}
elements.sent-2411-042: However, existing approaches assume that label spaces are identical across both domains, limiting their applicability in real-world scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-043: Open-set Domain Adaptation (OSDA) addresses scenarios where the target domain may contain classes not present in the source domain~\cite{panareda2017open,dong2024moosa,dong2024,li2021domain .}
elements.sent-2411-044: Various approaches have been proposed to tackle this challenge, including assigning target domain images to source categories while discarding unrelated target domain images~\cite{panareda2017open , and using adversarial training to separate unknown target samples~\cite{saito2018open,jang2022unknown .}}
elements.sent-2411-045: Separate to Adapt (STA) approach~\cite{liu2019separate progressively separates unknown and known class samples using a coarse-to-fine weighting mechanism and proposes evaluating OSDA on diverse levels of openness.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Rotation-based Open Set (ROS)~\cite{bucci2020effectiveness explores the use of self-supervised tasks such as rotation recognition for unknown class detection.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Adjustment and Alignment for Unbiased Open Set Domain Adaptation (ANNA)~\cite{li2023adjustment addresses semantic-level bias in OSDA by designing Front-Door Adjustment and Decoupled Causal Alignment modules.}
elements.sent-2411-048: However, these approaches all assume the availability of labeled source data, which can pose challenges due to privacy concerns in real applications.
elements.sent-2411-049: Source-free Domain Adaptation (SFDA) leverages only a source-trained model and unlabeled target data for adaptation to the target domain.
elements.sent-2411-050: SFDA approaches can be categorized into data-based and model-based methods~\cite{yu2023comprehensive .}
elements.sent-2411-051: One of the data-driven methods, SHOT, was introduced by Liang et al.~\cite{liang2020we .}
elements.sent-2411-052: It adapts a pre-trained source model via information maximization with self-supervised pseudo-labeling to implicitly align target domain representations to the source hypothesis.
elements.sent-2411-053: Building on this approach, subsequent works\cite{chu2022denoised,lee2022confidence,qu2022bmd refine the adaptation through self-training techniques.}
elements.sent-2411-054: Other works explore different training procedures.
elements.sent-2411-055: For example, historical Contrastive Learning (HCL) \cite{huang2021model compensates for the absence of source data by leveraging historical models and contrasting current and historical embeddings of target samples.}
elements.sent-2411-056: Some methods~\cite{yang2021generalized,yang2022attracting enforce consistency between local neighbors by considering local feature density, with Attract and Disperse (AaD)~\cite{yang2022attracting treating SFDA as an unsupervised clustering problem.}}
elements.sent-2411-057: Additionally, \citet{zhang2023class explore leveraging source model classifier weights as class prototypes to embed class relationships into a similarity measure for a target sample.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Source-free Open-set Domain Adaptation (SF-OSDA) extends SFDA to scenarios where the target domain contains novel classes not present in the source domain.
elements.sent-2411-059: While methods like SHOT~\cite{liang2020we , AaD~\cite{yang2022attracting , and Uncertainty-guided Source-free Domain Adaptation (U-SFAN) ~\cite{roy2022uncertainty have been adapted for SF-OSDA, they primarily focus on the known class semantics in the source domain, which can lead to suboptimal handling of target-private unknown classes.}}}
elements.sent-2411-060: Universal Domain Adaptation (UniDA) aims to handle domain shifts and label set differences between source and target domains, encompassing open, partial, and open-partial set scenarios~\cite{liang2021umad,qu2023upcycling,qu2024lead .}
elements.sent-2411-061: Recent SF-UniDA methods proposed one-vs-all clustering approaches~\cite{qu2023upcycling and subspace decomposition~\cite{qu2024lead to separate and identify common and private target classes in a source-free setup.}}
elements.sent-2411-062: Similarly, Progressive Graph Learning~\cite{luo2023source decomposes the target hypothesis space into shared and unknown subspaces for SF-OSDA.}
elements.sent-2411-063: However, current methods either require specific training for the source model to incorporate the unknown classes~\cite{kundu_cvpr_2020,kundu2020towards , which is usually impractical, or rely on thresholding a metric to distinguish known classes from unknown ones during training and inference, making the prediction sensitive to different thresholds.}

elements.sent-2411-064: For SF-OSDA, we are given a source pre-trained model $f^s_{\theta}$ and an unlabeled target domain with $n_t$ samples, denoted as $\mathcal{D _t = \{(x_i^t)\}_{i=1 ^{n_t}$, where $x_i^t \in \mathcal{X \subset \mathbb{R ^X$.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-069: The primary objective of SF-OSDA is to classify both unknown and known classes, relying exclusively on the target domain data and a pre-trained source model.
elements.sent-2411-070: The pre-trained model can be decomposed as $f^s_{\theta = h^s_{\theta \circ g^s_{\theta}$, where $h^s_{\theta : \mathbb{R ^X \rightarrow \mathbb{R ^D$ is a feature extractor and $g^s_{\theta : \mathbb{R ^D \rightarrow \mathbb{R ^K$ is the source classifier.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: Unlike previous works, which freeze the source classifier (e.g., SHOT) during adaptation, we propose training a new target classifier $g^t_{\theta}$ to explicitly account for target-private unknown classes.
elements.sent-2411-072: One of the challenges in open-set scenarios is the ability to distinguish known from unknown classes in the target domain.
elements.sent-2411-073: Different approaches have been proposed for distinguishing between known and unknown classes, including hand-crafted thresholding criteria and clustering strategies.
elements.sent-2411-074: However, paradigms such as vendor-to-client~\cite{kundu2020towards are more effective, as they incorporate an auxiliary out-of-distribution classifier during source training, enabling better handling of unknown classes in the target domain.}
elements.sent-2411-075: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to address this limitation by adapting the source classifier post hoc to include new decision boundaries for unknown classes.
elements.sent-2411-076: Our method enables the seamless adaptation of any off-the-shelf source pre-trained model to a target domain, even in the presence of novel classes.
elements.sent-2411-077: Motivated by the idea that learning from unknown class samples can improve performance in open-set scenarios, our objective is to simplify adaptation and eliminate the dependency on threshold-based methods during inference.
elements.sent-2411-078: Our proposed Recall and Refine framework for SF-OSDA consists of three main steps:.
elements.sent-2411-079: Synthetic Data Generation: Referring to step (b) in Figure~\ref{fig:main , synthetic feature points are generated for both known and unknown classes.}
elements.sent-2411-080: This involves optimizing target feature representations using entropy objectives.
elements.sent-2411-081: Target Classifier Training: Referring to step (c) in Figure~\ref{fig:main , the synthetic feature points are used to train a new target classifier $g_\theta^t$ with extended decision boundaries to accommodate unknown classes.}
elements.sent-2411-082: Target Domain Adaptation: The entire model is adapted using any off-the-shelf source-free domain adaptation methods (e.g., SHOT, AaD) on target domain data.
elements.sent-2411-083: This allows the model to (1) learn the semantics of both known and unknown classes in the target domain, (2) treat OSDA as a simple closed-set scenario, and (3) directly output predictions for unknown classes.
elements.sent-2411-084: The first step of our proposed approach involves generating synthetic features for both known and unknown classes using the source classifier $g^s_{\theta}$.
elements.sent-2411-085: Specifically, we optimize the target feature representation $\mathbf{z ^t = h^s_{\theta (x^t)$ to generate synthetic samples that exhibit low entropy for known classes and high entropy for the unknown class.}}
elements.sent-2411-086: We denote these optimized synthetic features as $\mathbf{z _{k ^{*t}$ and $\mathbf{z _{unk ^{*t}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: The unknown features are then clustered in $K'$ classes, and a new target classifier $g^t_{\theta}$ is introduced with $K+K'$ classes.
elements.sent-2411-088: In this section, we describe the process for obtaining feature representations for both known and unknown classes.
elements.sent-2411-089: We use standard gradient descent optimization to generate the desired feature representations.
elements.sent-2411-090: Synthetic Unknown Classes Generation: To effectively identify points near the source classifier's decision boundary, we aim to find $\mathbf{z ^{*t _{unk}$ that maximizes entropy while ensuring diverse feature representations, thereby reducing the risk of collapsing to a single-point representation.}}
elements.sent-2411-091: To prevent feature collapse, we introduce a variance regularization term in the form of a hinge function applied to the standard deviation of features across the batch dimension.
elements.sent-2411-092: Specifically, we initialize the optimization with a noisy version of the original features $\mathbf{z ^t$.}
elements.sent-2411-093: This process is formulated as follows: \begin{equation}\min_{\mathbf{z}^t} \quad -H(\sigma(g^s_{\theta}(\mathbf{z}^t))) + \lambda \cdot \max(0, 1 - \sqrt{\text{Var}(\mathbf{z}^t)}) ,\end{equation} where $H(p) = - \sum_{k=1 ^K p_k\log(p_k)$ represents the entropy, and $\sigma$ is the softmax activation function, and $\lambda$ was set to 1 for all the experiments.}
elements.sent-2411-094: After optimization, only the points satisfying $H(g^s_{\theta (\mathbf{z )) > 0.75 \cdot \log(K)$ (see Ablation section for threshold discussion) are considered as $\mathbf{z _{unk ^{*t}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-095: The selected features $\mathbf{z ^{*t _{unk}$ are then clustered into $K'$ unknown classes using K-means.}}
elements.sent-2411-096: Each cluster is assigned a pseudo-label corresponding to a new class index, $\hat{y _{unk ^{*t \in \{K+1,..., K+K'\}$, representing the specific unknown class assigned to the synthetic features.}}}
elements.sent-2411-097: These synthetic features and their associated pseudo-labels ($\mathbf{z ^{*t _{unk}$, $\hat{y _{unk ^{*t}$) will be used in the subsequent training of the target classifier.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-098: This approach is motivated by the observation in the literature~\cite{lampert2009learning that it is possible to generate meaningful semantics for novel classes using known classes.}
elements.sent-2411-099: Synthetic Known Classes Generation: A similar optimization approach is employed to generate synthetic data points for the known classes.
elements.sent-2411-100: The optimization is performed iteratively $K$ times, once for each known class $k$ (where $k \in [1, ..., K]$).
elements.sent-2411-101: The objective is to minimize the cross-entropy for each class directly from $\mathbf{z ^t$.}
elements.sent-2411-102: The optimization problem for generating a sample for class $k$ is defined as: \begin{equation}\min_{\mathbf{z}^t} \quad \mathcal{L}_{CE}(g^s_{\theta}(\mathbf{z}^t), I_k) + \lambda \cdot \max(0, 1 - \sqrt{\text{Var}(\mathbf{z}^t)}),\end{equation} where $I_k$ is the identity function for the $k$-th class (i.e., a one-hot vector), and $\lambda$ controls the regularization term, set to 1 in all experiments.
elements.sent-2411-103: After optimization, only the points satisfying $\mathcal{L _{CE (g^s_{\theta (\mathbf{z ), I_k) < 0.25 \cdot \log(K)$ (see Ablation section for threshold discussion) are considered as $\mathbf{z _{k ^{*t}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-104: Each selected synthetic feature $\mathbf{z _{k ^{*t}$ is assigned the pseudo-label $\hat{y _k^{*t = k$, forming the pairs ($\mathbf{z _{k ^{*t}$,$\hat{y _k^{*t $).}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-105: These synthetic data points and their corresponding pseudo-labels are then used to train the target classifier.
elements.sent-2411-106: By iteratively generating feature points for each known class, our method enhances the decision boundaries without requiring access to the original source data or labels.

elements.sent-2411-164: Our method significantly improves existing SF-OSDA techniques, as evidenced by the consistent performance gains across all datasets.
elements.sent-2411-165: The key advantage of RRDA lies in its novel approach to handling unknown classes.
elements.sent-2411-166: Unlike previous methods that rely on thresholding and discard unknown class data during adaptation, RRDA actively learns the semantics of unknown classes through our adaptive target classifier, which evolves to accommodate the unknown class distribution.
elements.sent-2411-167: Furthermore, the consistent performance gains with both AaD and SHOT demonstrate RRDA's versatility.
elements.sent-2411-168: These results underscore the importance of explicitly modeling unknown classes in open-set domain adaptation, rather than treating them as outliers to be discarded.

elements.sent-2411-201: In this work, we introduce Recall and Refine for Domain Adaptation (RRDA), a simple but effective framework for SF-OSDA.
elements.sent-2411-202: RRDA enables the successful adaptation of off-the-shelf source pre-trained models to target domains, effectively addressing both distribution and category shift problems.
elements.sent-2411-203: RRDA achieves this by introducing a new target classifier that aids in classifying and learning the semantics of both known and unknown classes.
elements.sent-2411-204: This approach enables the direct use of source-free adaptation methods designed for closed-set scenarios in open-set contexts.
elements.sent-2411-205: Extensive experiments on three challenging benchmarks demonstrate that RRDA significantly outperforms existing SF-OSDA methods and even surpasses OSDA methods that have access to the source domain.
elements.sent-2411-206: Future work could explore its potential for continuous adaptation in the setup where new classes appear over time.
elements.sent-2411-207: This supplementary material contains additional information and experiments to support our main paper on open-set domain adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-208: Specifically, it includes the pseudo-code for Synthetic Data Generation and an additional Ablation Study on the target classifier initialization.
elements.sent-2411-209: We present the pseudo-code for our synthetic data generation algorithms used for training the target classifier:.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Synthetic Known Classes Generation} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE features, classifier, classDesired, numClass, numSteps \STATE points $\leftarrow$ Clone(features) \STATE points.requiresGrad $\leftarrow$ true \STATE optimizer $\leftarrow$ Adam([points], lr=0.001) \FOR{step = 1 \TO numSteps} \STATE scores $\leftarrow$ classifier(points) \STATE stdLoss $\leftarrow$ Mean(ReLU(1 - sqrt(Var(points) + 1e-4))) \STATE entropies $\leftarrow$ CrossEntropyLoss(scores, classDesired) \STATE loss $\leftarrow$ Mean(entropies) + stdLoss \STATE Backpropagate(loss) and Update(optimizer) \ENDFOR \STATE idx $\leftarrow$ FindIndices(entropies $<$ 0.25 log(numClass)) \RETURN points[idx], classDesired \end{algorithmic}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Synthetic Unknown Classes Generation} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE features, classifier, numClass, kPrime, numSteps \STATE points $\leftarrow$ Clone(features) \STATE points.requiresGrad $\leftarrow$ true \STATE optimizer $\leftarrow$ Adam([points], lr=0.001) \FOR{step = 1 \TO numSteps} \STATE scores $\leftarrow$ classifier(points) \STATE stdLoss $\leftarrow$ Mean(ReLU(1 - sqrt(Var(points) + 1e-3))) \STATE probs $\leftarrow$ Softmax(scores) \STATE entropies $\leftarrow$ -Sum(probs * log(probs + 1e-8)) \STATE loss $\leftarrow$ -Mean(entropies) + stdLoss \STATE Backpropagate(loss) and Update(optimizer) \ENDFOR \STATE idx $\leftarrow$ FindIndices(entropies $>$ 0.75 log(numClass)) \STATE kmeans $\leftarrow$ KMeans(nClusters=kPrime).fit(points[idx]) \RETURN points[idx], kmeans.labels + numClass \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-210: The Synthetic Known Classes Generation function optimizes synthetic data points to be confidently classified into known classes shared between source and target domains.
elements.sent-2411-211: It minimizes cross-entropy loss while maintaining point diversity through a standard deviation loss term.
elements.sent-2411-212: The Synthetic Unknown Classes Generation function creates synthetic data points for potential unknown classes in the target domain.
elements.sent-2411-213: It maximizes entropy to generate points that are difficult for the classifier to categorize, simulating unknown classes.
elements.sent-2411-214: The function also uses k-means clustering to assign synthetic labels to these unknown class points.
elements.sent-2411-215: Both functions use gradient descent optimization to iteratively refine the synthetic points.
elements.sent-2411-216: The final selection of points is based on entropy thresholds, ensuring that known class points have low entropy (high confidence) and unknown class points have high entropy (low confidence).

elements.sent-2411-217: We present an ablation study comparing the impact of known class initialization on the final performance of RRDA on the Office-31 dataset.
elements.sent-2411-218: In our approach, we first generate synthetic points for both known and unknown classes.
elements.sent-2411-219: These synthetic points are then used to train the target classifier before performing the adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-220: Our main paper uses source initialization for the known classes in the target classifier before this training process.
elements.sent-2411-221: As shown in Table \ref{tab:results_office_31 , source initialization of the known classes yields a consistent improvement over random initialization.}
elements.sent-2411-222: Importantly, both SHOT+RRDA variants (random and source initialization of the target classifier) significantly outperform the baseline SHOT-O method, with average improvements of 5.0\% and 5.6\%, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-223: These findings demonstrate that our synthetic data generation process is robust and effective.
elements.sent-2411-224: Even when the target classifier is randomly initialized before training with the synthetic points, our method achieves strong performance.
elements.sent-2411-225: This underscores the quality of the generated synthetic points for both known and unknown classes.
elements.sent-2411-226: Existing SF-OSDA methods typically rely on thresholding the prediction entropy of a sample to identify it as either a known or unknown class, but fail to explicitly learn discriminative features for the target-private unknown classes.
elements.sent-2411-228: Second, we adapt the entire model to the target domain, addressing both domain shifts and distinguishability to unknown classes.
elements.sent-2411-229: Any off-the-shelf source-free domain adaptation method (e.g.\ SHOT, AaD) can be seamlessly integrated into our framework at this stage.
elements.sent-2411-231: Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (UDA)~\citep{ben2010theory,ganin2015unsupervised,long2015learning adapts a model from a labeled source domain to an unlabeled target domain~\citep{oza2023unsupervised , effectively addressing the issue of domain shift where the source and target distributions differ.}}
elements.sent-2411-232: UDA strategies typically align feature distributions between domains using metric learning techniques~\citep{long2015learning,kang2019contrastive or adversarial training~\citep{ganin2015unsupervised, tzeng2017adversarial,luo2019taking , and more recently, self-training approaches~\citep{sun2022safe, Hoyer_2023_CVPR, Zhu_2023_CVPR .}}}
elements.sent-2411-233: Despite their success, most current domain adaptation approaches operate under the assumption of a shared label set between the source and target domains (i.e., $\mathcal{C _s = \mathcal{C _t$), referred to as Closed-set Domain Adaptation~\citep{saenko2010adapting .}}}
elements.sent-2411-234: In contrast, Open-set Domain Adaptation (OSDA) extends the target label space beyond that of the source domain (i.e., $\mathcal{C _s\subset \mathcal{C _t$)~\citep{saito2018open,liu2019separate , thereby adding complexity to the DA task.}}}
elements.sent-2411-235: OSDA aims to align target samples from known classes with those from the source domain while effectively identifying target samples belonging to categories not observed in the source domain, referred to as unknown classes~\citep{panareda2017open,bucci2020effectiveness,jang2022unknown .}
elements.sent-2411-236: Various criteria based on instance-level predictions have been proposed, including entropy-based~\citep{feng2021open,saito2020universal and confidence-based~\citep{saito2021ovanet,fu2020learning methods.}}
elements.sent-2411-237: To address this, source-free adaptation methods~\citep{fang2024source have emerged, enabling adaptation without reliance on labeled source data~\citep{kim2021domain,kundu2020universal,li2020model .}}
elements.sent-2411-238: Although some Source-free Domain Adaptation (SF-DA) methods have demonstrated effectiveness in addressing SF-OSDA for classification tasks~\citep{liang2020we,yang2022attracting,wan2024unveiling , semantic segmentation~\citep{choe2024open , and graph applications~\citep{wang2024open , they primarily focus on the semantics of known classes in the source domain, often overlooking the crucial aspect of novel-class semantics.}}}
elements.sent-2411-239: To address these limitations, we propose Recall and Refine (RRDA), a novel SF-OSDA framework that explicitly learns representations for target-private unknown classes by generating synthetic target-domain features.
elements.sent-2411-240: Our approach leverages the key insight that a source classifier trained only on known classes yields high-entropy (highly uncertain) predictions for unknown samples and low-entropy (highly confident) predictions for known ones.
elements.sent-2411-241: We optimize synthetic features to exhibit these entropy characteristics, cluster the resulting high-entropy features into $K'$ pseudo-unknown classes, and refine the source classifier’s decision boundaries accordingly to accommodate novel categories.
elements.sent-2411-242: This small change recasts open-set adaptation as a closed-set problem, allowing any off-the-shelf source-free domain adaptation method (e.g., SHOT~\citep{liang2020we , AaD~\citep{yang2022attracting ) to be seamlessly integrated for joint domain-shift and unknown-class separation.}}
elements.sent-2411-243: Extensive experiments on three SF-OSDA benchmarks demonstrate that RRDA significantly outperforms existing methods.

elements.sent-2411-335: Recent trends in domain adaptation have focused on leveraging foundation models to tackle the domain shift problem \cite{tang2024source,zara2023autolabel,singha2023ad .}
elements.sent-2411-336: To demonstrate RRDA's flexibility and compatibility with modern vision-language models, we conducted additional experiments integrating our approach with CLIP \cite{radford2021learning .}
elements.sent-2411-337: For our experimental setup, we used the Office-Home dataset.
elements.sent-2411-338: We didn't finetune the model, and considered 25 shared classes.
elements.sent-2411-339: Our CLIP configuration included both zero-shot classification using text embeddings of "a photo of [CLASS]" prompts, and our method initialized with CLIP-ViT-B/32 image encoder.
elements.sent-2411-340: We augmented this with entropy-based unknown detection (threshold = 0.5 × log(25)), as we considered only the known classes as prompts.
elements.sent-2411-341: For our approach, we leveraged text-guided synthetic feature generation for known classes, while employing a novel K-means clustering strategy (K'=K) on high-entropy features for unknown modeling.
elements.sent-2411-342: Specifically, after identifying samples with high entropy, we optimized these features to maximize their entropy further.
elements.sent-2411-343: We then applied K-means clustering to these optimized high-entropy features, creating K' distinct unknown prototype clusters.
elements.sent-2411-344: This approach allows us to model the unknown space with multiple centroids rather than a single homogeneous "unknown" class, enabling more nuanced decision boundaries and better separation between known and unknown samples.
elements.sent-2411-345: Table~\ref{tab:clip_results presents results across different domains, showing that RRDA significantly enhances CLIP's performance for open-set domain adaptation.}
elements.sent-2411-346: While zero-shot CLIP provides a strong baseline (57.6\% average HOS), RRDA integration improves performance by 13.4 percentage points (71.0\% average HOS).

elements.sent-2411-347: RRDA offers a robust framework for SF-OSDA with several promising directions for future enhancement.
elements.sent-2411-348: Our current implementation uses a fixed relationship between known and unknown classes (K'=K) for simplicity, which has proven effective across diverse benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-349: Future work could explore adaptive strategies for determining the optimal K' based on target domain characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-350: The performance depends on the quality of synthetic features, where optimizing feature generation techniques could further improve adaptation results.
elements.sent-2411-351: From a broader perspective, RRDA advances privacy-preserving machine learning by enabling effective adaptation without source data access.
elements.sent-2411-352: This has significant implications for sensitive domains like healthcare and industrial systems, where data sharing is restricted by privacy regulations or proprietary concerns.
elements.sent-2411-353: By improving classification of unknown classes, RRDA also enhances the safety and reliability of deployed systems in open-world environments where novel classes may appear unexpectedly.
elements.sent-2411-354: Additional promising research directions include extending RRDA to continual learning scenarios where new unknown classes appear over time, and exploring its application in multi-modal settings to further extend its practical impact across diverse application domains.
